verses.commentary: Faith in Christ and favoritism are incompatible. Believers in the glorious Lord Jesus must not show partiality (prosōpolēmpsia, προσωπολημψία). The glory of Christ condemns worldly status games.
Scenario: rich man in fine clothes, poor man in filthy clothes. Both enter the assembly. The test of faith is how we treat those who differ in social status.

Special attention to the rich, contempt for the poor. Here is a good seat versus stand over there or sit on the floor. The church mirrors the world's values when it honors wealth and dishonors poverty.

Have you not discriminated among yourselves? Become judges with evil thoughts (dialogismoi ponēroi, διαλογισμοί πονηροί). Favoritism reveals wicked judgment rooted in worldly values, not God's kingdom.

God chose the poor in the world's eyes to be rich in faith. Kingdom economics invert worldly wealth. The poor are heirs of the kingdom promised to those who love God. Love for God, not material wealth, determines inheritance.
You dishonor the poor. Yet the rich exploit you and drag you into court. Irony: the church honors those who oppress them. Worldly wisdom is foolishness.
The rich blaspheme the noble name you bear. They dishonor Christ. Why honor those who dishonor the One you follow? Allegiance to Christ demands different values.
The royal law: love your neighbor as yourself (Leviticus 19:18). If you keep this, you do well. Love is the fulfillment of the law. Favoritism violates love.
Favoritism is sin. You are convicted by the law as lawbreakers. Partiality is not a minor issue; it is a violation of God's law, a transgression with consequences.
Breaking one commandment makes you guilty of all. The law is a unified whole. You cannot selectively obey. Stumbling at one point reveals lawbreaking at the root.
God said do not commit adultery and do not murder. Breaking one makes you a lawbreaker. The issue is not degree of sin but the fact of rebellion against God's authority.
Speak and act as those judged by the law that gives freedom. Gospel obedience is not bondage but liberty. We will give account. Live accordingly.
Judgment without mercy for those who show no mercy. Mercy triumphs over judgment. Those who have received mercy must extend mercy. The merciful receive mercy (Matthew 5:7).
Can faith without deeds save? The question expects the answer: no. James is not contradicting Paul; he is exposing false faith. True faith produces works. Workless faith is dead faith.
Scenario: a brother or sister lacks clothes and food. Concrete need. Faith must respond to visible suffering.
Saying go in peace, keep warm and fed, but doing nothing is useless. Words without action are empty. Faith that does not meet needs is not faith.
Faith by itself, without action, is dead (nekra, νεκρά). Dead faith cannot save. Living faith produces works. The two are inseparable.
Show me your faith without deeds. Impossible. Faith is invisible; works make it visible. I will show you my faith by my deeds. Works are the evidence of faith.
Belief in one God is good. Even demons believe and shudder. Intellectual assent is not saving faith. Orthodoxy without obedience is demonic religion.
Foolish person, do you need evidence that faith without deeds is useless (argē, ἀργή)? Barren, unproductive. James will provide Old Testament proof.
Abraham was considered righteous by what he did when he offered Isaac. Genesis 22. Abraham's faith was proven genuine by radical obedience. The act demonstrated the reality of his trust in God.
Faith and actions worked together. Faith was made complete (teleioō, τελειόω) by works. Not that works add to faith, but they bring faith to its full expression. Works are faith's fruit.

Genesis 15:6 fulfilled: Abraham believed God, and it was credited to him as righteousness. Paul uses this to prove justification by faith (Romans 4:3). James uses it to show faith produces obedience. No contradiction: different emphasis.

A person is justified by works, not by faith alone. Justified here means demonstrated righteous, not declared righteous. James addresses false professors; Paul addresses legalists. Both agree: true faith works.

Rahab the prostitute was considered righteous for her actions. She hid the spies and sent them away safely (Joshua 2). A Gentile woman of ill repute is justified by risky obedience. Faith defies social norms to obey God.
As the body without spirit is dead, so faith without deeds is dead. Powerful analogy. A corpse is not living; faithless works are not faith. Faith and works are as inseparable as body and spirit.
